era_context: The second year of Antoninus Pius's reign; the Roman Empire continues its period of relative peace and stability. The Antonine Wall construction in Britain is underway. In China, the Eastern Han Dynasty experiences continued court factionalism. The Parthian Empire remains the dominant power in the Near East and Central Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited specific documentation for this year)

events.title: Antonine Wall construction continues in Britain
events.description: Construction of the Antonine Wall progresses under Governor Quintus Lollius Urbicus. The wall is built with turf and stone, stretching across central Scotland. This year represents the continuation of the ambitious building project initiated in 140 CE (or possibly 139 CE, depending on source interpretation). The construction involves significant Roman military resources and demonstrates the empire's continued investment in frontier consolidation.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Antonine Wall

events.certainty_note: The exact year of construction initiation is debated (139–142 CE range). Archaeological evidence provides the most reliable dating, but precision to a single year is difficult.

events.title: No major documented events for this year in surviving sources
events.description: 139 CE is not marked by any major documented historical events in surviving primary or secondary sources. This reflects the general sparsity of year-by-year documentation for the 2nd century CE outside of major political crises or military campaigns. The year falls within the broader era of Antonine stability in Rome and continued Eastern Han decline in China, but no specific events of historical significance are recorded.
events.certainty_note: The absence of documented events is itself a documented fact; this reflects the nature of historical sources rather than an absence of historical activity.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The sparsity of documentation for 139 CE is consistent with the broader pattern of 2nd-century sources, which tend to record major events rather than year-by-year developments.
historiographic_note: 139 CE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The year falls between major documented events and is not marked by any crisis, succession, or military campaign significant enough to be recorded in detail. This is typical for the 2nd century CE, where documentation is episodic rather than continuous. The Antonine Wall construction may have continued this year, but sources do not specify. Chinese sources are similarly silent on specific events for 139 CE. The lack of documentation does not indicate historical inactivity but rather reflects the selective nature of ancient historical recording.

graph_edges.note: Antonine Wall construction continues across both years
